ranked.title: Sennheiser HDB 630 review - Better than Bose, Sony, and Apple?
ranked.description:
🛒 Buy the Sennheiser HDB 630 on Amazon: https://soundg.co/tmsyrt
Audio mainstay Sennheiser has long been in the conversation when it comes to the best-sounding headphones, but it’s definitely struggled a bit when it comes to active noise canceling (ANC) headphones. That being said, the new Sennheiser HDB 630, these cans have some big shoes to fill — and some pretty big gaps to cover. Let’s see how they do.

🛒 Buy the Sennheiser HDB 630 on Amazon: https://soundg.co/u9y7i6
🧐Read our full review (See full lab results & Mic Samples): https://www.soundguys.com/sennheiser-hdb-630-review-audiophile-anc-at-a-cost-146569/

Bought them, happy so far, definitely the best set of wireless ANC headphones I've had yet - they replace a very well worn set of Momentum 3's that had started to have battery trouble. I tested them blind against some other options with a friend writing down my scores for each set before I made my decision. I've stuck with the neutral EQ for the moment whilst I get accustomed to them - but the increase in detail is obvious and the bass is considerably less bloated. They don't feel or look especially 'special' for the money but I'm happy enough with the build quality and the BTD 700 has already proved useful adding wireless listening to my desktop PC (the low latency mode is pretty good for gaming too). ANC performance is 'ok' - there's no doubt that the XM6's offer allot more ANC so if I had a regular commute/travel in noisy areas I'd probably of gone for those. That said they were good enough for me to miss a good 10 minutes of annoyed meowing from my fairly loud and bossy cat! 🤣 Do they beat wired listening? - well I'd say not, if I want a critical sitting down listening session I have my still not especially posh (I can't claim to be a true audiophile) but brilliant for the money Philips Fidelio X2HR.

After many hours of use, I can say that the HDB 630s sound fantastic, getting better every day. I use them with my custom equalizer, adjusted to my hearing and without the bass boost, and the sound is excellent. I own more than 40 different headphones, and the HDB 630s offer exceptional sound quality for their price. And with the BTD 700, the volume on my PC is phenomenal, plus it has 24-bit/96kHz resolution.

I’ve had the XM6 for about six months and have used them almost every day. In between, I bought the Bose QC Ultra 2 but returned it because the sound quality is roughly the same, but the touch controls on the Sony are significantly better. I’ve had the Sennheiser HDB 630 for just under two weeks, and I’ll probably get flak for this comment, but LDAC or supposed 24/96 capabilities aside, the Sennheiser is in a league of its own in terms of sound; it sounds worlds better at 16/48 with a lossless source (Apple Music for me) than the other two, even when using AptX/Adaptive with the included dongle. Sony and Bose are fantastic when it comes to noise cancellation and using them on the go, but if you want sound quality comparable to high-end wired headphones, the Sennheiser is unbeatable.

ranked.transcript: I remember the days when people used to laugh at the thought of spending $500 on headphones. Now it's just a fact of life if you want the latest A&C cans. So what happens when an audio file company matches the likes of Sony, Apple, and Bose in price? You get the Sennheiser HDB 630? Already it's an audio file darling. But do the headphones really deserve the praise they're getting, or are they yet another product competing out of their league? I tested these in our labs for a couple weeks and have the answer. Let's meet the HDB 630. Sennheiser's latest headphones will instantly look familiar to headphone enthusiasts because they share a lot of common elements with the beloved Sennheiser Momentum 4 Wireless. The new headphones have a similar shape, the same matte exterior, and the biggest outward change that you can see is the decision to go with a fake but convincing leather-like material to replace the cloth headband and padding and also much beefier ear pads. Under the hood, there's a lot of similar components like an internal DAC that can decode 24-bit 96 kHz files over USB or wireless sources. Additionally, there's a similar ANC system along with similarly huge battery. Like the older headphones, the HDB 630 has no ingress protection rating, so keep it away from moisture if you can. But these headphones aren't the Momentum 4 wireless, [music] and it's pretty obvious that's the case shortly after you put them on. The increased mass of 311 gram up from the 295 gram might be hardly noticeable, but it's because of the significantly beefed up padding. Said padding is very comfortable, and I had no issues listening to the headphones for an entire workday at a time. The deep pads meet glasses wearers with something that doesn't clamp hard down onto their skulls, and while it does tend to trap heat, that's pretty standard for the A&C headphone experience. The chassis is mainly plastic, which is probably necessary to keep the weight down. You can adjust the headband by using the friction rod design. And since it doesn't have exposed hinges or pinchy bits, those of you with long hair shouldn't get their locks caught in the yolk or chassis too often. The headphones come with a hard travel case covered in fabric, the mesh pockets to contain the dongle, 3.5 to 2.5 mm analog cable, USBC cable, and the folded flat HDB 630. Given the number of little bits you can lose on a given day, this is a godsend. [music] Though the Sennheiser HDB 630 doesn't make its name on features, it definitely has no shortage of them. The Smart Control Plus app is the hub for your headphone settings with the ability to locate them when they're lost, adjust A&C or transparency levels, controls, equalizer presets, you get the idea. But the thing I'm most excited about is the new parametric equalizer. Though it's a bit on the nerdy or difficult side for most people, enthusiasts will be able to add up to five of their own filters to make the HDB 630 match their preferences. And given the excellent frequency response of the headphones, it should be very easy to achieve the results that fit you best. A+ Sennheiser. [music] A plus. Well, I would have liked a system for people to share their presets like nothing does. Each filter in the app labels what the filter characteristics are exactly. meaning that with only five filters available, it shouldn't be too hard to list out what settings you used. Nothing's on the other hand, yeah, needs a bit of a heavy hand, said the guy with the most popular preset on the Nothing X platform. Heyo. For those of you who have all sorts of different sources, like smartphones, digital audio players, vinyl, any disc, the Sennheiser HDB 630 can connect to virtually anything by virtue of the fact that it uses analog, digital, and wireless sources. And for times when your wireless source doesn't support the best codecs available to the headphones, you can use the included BTD700 dongle to give your computer or smartphone the capability to use ADEX adaptive. Not too shabby. But the news isn't all sunshine and roses. For example, while the BTD700 is capable of broadcasting via Oracast and AEX lossless, the Bluetooth 5point headphones themselves don't seem to support these. Not the end of the world, but something to note. The headphones do support AEX HD. However, though it's quite old, even by today's standards, still good, just old. Without the dongle, sometimes the headphones can get a little confused when you're listening to a secondary device like a computer and your smartphone tries to connect to the headphones, occasionally swapping the audio stream, but that doesn't happen often enough for it to be worth worrying about. In our standardized battery test, the Sennheiser HTB 630 lasted 53 hours and 46 minutes with the ANC on. We test all headphones level matched to the same listening level with a test track that contains real music peaking at 75 dB SPL. So, if you crank your tunes, you may enjoy a bit less time. If you talk on the phone a lot, same deal. Use more features, get less battery. This battery life is stellar. While it may not take the crown for the best ever tested, it is way more than the Sony WH100XM 6, more than the Bose Quiet Comfort Ultra, and vastly more than the Apple AirPods Max. Now, microphone performance is difficult to be fair to when you realize that there are external factors that can affect the outcomes, but overall, the Sennheiser HDB 630 equates itself fairly well. You can hear our samples on the review with recordings that offer a baseline, office noise, street noise, reverberant bass, and simulated wind. Go check that out. Hearing is believing. Decide for yourself. If there is one notable downside to using the Sennheiser HTB 630, it's the active noise cancelling. And it's frustrating because while overall it measures fairly well, the active unit of the headphones is the weak point here. Despite the fact that you can achieve loudness reduction of outside noise by about 80 to 83% on average depending on fit, the headphones aren't able to attenuate noise well in a really important range. Specifically, [music] 300 Hz to 1.5 kHz will be attenuated by up to 9 dB less effectively than the Sony WH100XM6 does. Now, this was an issue for the older Sennheiser Momentum 4 wireless as well. And while it's not too bad, it's definitely something to consider when you're deciding whether sound quality or A&C is more important to you. If you look at the A&C effect for both headphones without taking isolation into account, they're really quite similar. And it goes to paint the picture that the main upgrade here is really just the ear pads. The other issue here is that the level of noise attenuation you can achieve should vary significantly as the improvement for the HDB 630 offers over the previous headphones is the isolation like I just said and not the A&C. So even minor fit issues can drop performance even further. So, if you wear glasses, sunglasses, hearing aid, earrings, a hat, have a ton of hair, or really anything that could interrupt the pads for making a good seal on your head, you can expect that these headphones will struggle more with the outside noise than something like the Sony WH100XM6. That said, this level of A&C is more than adequate for a flight, and the only time I wished for a better performance was in a segment of the Vancouver Sky Train I dub the screech zone. This section of track is underground and the noise reverberates off the walls insanely loudly and right in that divot of that attenuation profile. This particular noise may as well have been concocted in a lab to be the most annoying foe for the HDB 630s ANC unit. So, it's hardly fair to knock it too hard, but it is something you might experience. Now, here's the meat of what audio files have been waiting for in terms of sound quality. All I can really say is damn. Damn, Sennheiser knows it stuff, but you don't often get A&C headphones that sound great. These do exactly that. While they really don't follow our preference curve in the highs, that's okay. The bass and mids are very important and keep up a trend of recent Sennheiser headphones of really nailing the fundamentals. Sure, they might sound a little dark leaning depending on your seal, but no matter what kind of fit you get, the sound is inoffensive at worst and really, really good at best. I suppose there is that weird hiccup between 1 and 2 kHz, but I believe that to be a consequence of the charts smoothing and a minor channel balance issue, as the raw results are much less poor here. Bass isn't overblown. Highs don't violently play jump rope with any target curve. And the only major deviation we can see is a narrow dip, which you really shouldn't hear all that much. Because of this, if you have different preferences, you can use the equalizer with very basic shelf or peak filters to achieve the results you want without nuking the sound quality. Now, if you're truly disturbed by this deviation in the highs from our preference curve, our old friends Floyd Tulle and Shaun Olive have some words to live by. only use the Harmon target as a guideline or reference when comparing relative differences among different headphones. While most people like it, it is not the last word on what sounds good. [music] What sounds good to you is ultimately what matters. [music] As an aside, if you want to learn more about audio, great place to start is sound reproduction, acoustics, and psycho acoustics of loudspeakers, rooms, and headphones out now. Dr. Dr. Sean Olive and Todd Welty made some pretty big additions to this book and uh notably for headphones, too. So, if you want to go check it out, link in the description. And who knows, maybe you'll see something that you recognize in that headphone section. I saw some consternation on Reddit about this. And while I could fill hours of YouTube videos on the subject of hard data versus soft data, data visualization, and why, it's often a matter of choosing the options that conflict with your mission the least rather than something that has an objectively perfect answer. You have to remember that the review exists to contextualize the observations we have in the lab and attempt to translate what that means for literal millions [music] of people. I'm not trying to tell you that you personally will absolutely like this. I'm saying that these headphones, the Sennheiser HDB 630, has a reasonable enough sound as a starting point to achieve what you want with minimal effort, if any at all. So, does the Sennheiser HDB 630 perform well enough for the price? Should you consider buying these headphones? Yes. That is, of course, unless you really, really, really, really need a top flight A and C. Otherwise, I'd get something else.

watch_points: 개인적인 음질 선호도에 맞는 EQ 조정
positive_signals: 조절 가능한 EQ에 대한 긍정적 피드백
negative_signals: ANC 성능 부족에 대한 반복적인 언급
가격 대비 경쟁력 부족에 대한 언급

reviewer_claims: Sennheiser HDB 630은 고음질 ANC 헤드폰으로, 경쟁 모델과 비교해 음질이 우수하다.
ANC 성능은 평균 수준이며, 특정 주파수대에서 약간의 약점을 보인다.

ranked.title: Everyone’s Hyping the Sennheiser HDB 630 — Are They Right?
ranked.description:
Sennheiser’s new HDB 630 is being called the best-sounding wireless ANC headphone yet—but does it actually live up to the hype? In this review, we dive into its sound quality, comfort, app features, and how it stacks up against Sony, Bose, and Focal’s competition.

Buy the Sennheiser HDB 630: https://headphones.com/products/sennheiser-hdb-630-wireless-headphones
Check out a written summary of this video: https://headphones.com/blogs/reviews/sennheiser-hdb-630-review-its-not-just-about-the-sound

comments:
Just a couple of notes:
-Folks are wondering if the HDB 630 competes with wired headphones. Yes, it absolutely does, and it actually beats many of them in terms of just sound quality alone. Some people aren't ready to hear this but... wireless headphones that are active with DSP give them all the potential in the world to sound significantly better than most passively tuned options. With that said, I still think you can get similar or better sound quality for the price if you don't need ANC for example. Audeze Maxwell is competitive here. It's not 'better' outright but in the ballpark, and that really should be seen as praise for the HDB 630. Same thing for the good wired headphones out there like the FT1 or the K371. To put it bluntly, the HDB 630 absolutely dunks on the majority of wired closed back headphones and that's because of DSP.
-Additional connectivity stuff: This is an always active headphone. So even if you run it with the analog cable, the headphone still needs to be turned on. This makes sense, because this is how it achieves its tuning.
-As others have said, you can also use it with its USB-C cable without obviously noticeable latency if you don't want to use it wirelessly.

Really great review! Im really thinking about snagging these. My go to for a while now has been the ATH-M50xbts, just cause i like a more neutral sound and they've done the job so i havent felt the need to upgrade. I did try a pair of sennheiser pxc 1s in between that time for a while and I remember being fond of those. Got a pair of wxms for travel as well. Would be interested in giving these a shot though as more of a daily driver.

Thank you for such detailed review. Have you considered reviewing the Edifier Stax S5 ? I’d love to see your review of the those headphones! Would appreciate your opinion. They’re wireless planar cans that are supposed to sound as good as some wired audiophile ones. I’m not really into ANC, just looking for nice wireless headphones for home listening to different types of music. I’ve done some research, and these two seem like top contenders.
I'm supremely happy with mine. One of the best purchases this year.

love your videos and channel- outstanding content.  Curious on a sound basis - how you compare these to the Audeze Maxwells.  I know the Maxwells are heavy and have no ANC.  On a sound basis, assuming you adjust the EQ to your liking, what is best in your opinion: Maxwells or Sen HDB630 (or even Focal Bathysmg)?  tx !

I forgot how high quality your reviews are. Amazing job, every assessment is explained, most of the time, with data, every category is covered... Many thanks.
Sennheiser is Sennheiser and it's a good thing for all of us
AKG N9 delivered dongle long time ago and it was great, too bad you didnt cover it.
Fantastic job man. Could you review the JBL One Tour M3 please

I use mine mostly at my computer and I've been having issues with it registering ghost touches to the side panel, randomly starting music playback despite not having my hands anywhere near the device. similarly, I can't adjust it without also sending a gesture command or two. it's actually pretty annoying.
Thanks for the very comprehensive review. Can we find the .csv file of the frequency response you measured somewhere?

My thoughts from owning for a couple of weeks. Really enjoy the sound. Much more clarity. I agree with the thought of perhaps slightly more padding on headband. A slightly larger cup would be good. They are already larger, I don't mind, and just a tad larger would be good. I would bay a little more for metal frame parts, but not a deal killer. Clamping force felt a bit much for my somewhat larger size head early on, but better over time. The ANC is not quite on par with other headphones I have, but good. Agree with the comments on sound quality vs. ANC performance tradeoff. I am accustomed to having ANC on when on calls. The transparency mode flip when I take a call I could do without. Phone audio does on occasion feel more canny. The battery life is awaesome. Overall I love them!
i had the opportunity to listen to this back to back with the bathys mg. wish i did it.
the AKG N5 and N9 had a better dongle about 1-2 years ago with LC3+ Codec
We’re waiting for the open-back version with the same tech. We only use over-ear headphones indoors, and for that, open-back is always the better choice for us.
Can you review the Bowers and Wilkins PX8 S2 and PX7 S3?
Great review! Thanks for that. Any comparison to the Mark Levinson 5909?

thanks Andrew
i think quite some people want you to elaborate on vs maxwell
So tempted to get this, but already have a master and dynamic MW75, which i quite like after EQ, and the build is way more premium. Don't think I can justify the purchase.
would you mind sharing your EQ settings?
My favorite headphone of 2025 and my favorite wireless headphone so far - period. Excellent job @Sennheiser!
Share your EQ, please! I'm shocked (and somehow disappointed) with so many reviewers praising its EQ capabilities but not sharing what you're doing.

Great review, but I really wish you'd show uncompensated graphs too - just find it easier to read, and yes its due to being used to reading raw, but I also don't want to 'get used to' a compensation to a target that almost certainly will change eventually, making prior ones outdated. But suppose its personal taste/opinion.

Great review. As mentioned by other though, you missed out talking about a very strong feature. USB-C DAC. Solves latency fully for gaming and lossless aswell. It charges the headphones while using aswell. Making these the best one in all solution that is out there to date.

I got a pair and the HD700 thing is, wow. Combined with the headphone, I played the regular tracks and I heard things I never heard before from something I played 100 times. The sound isolation is insane

I saw so many good reviews on this pair of headphones, I got it ordered last week and I was scared I would not get it anytime soon because from morning to afternoon it went basically out of stock everywhere. Luckily I got the mail last friday that the delivery company got it and it should arrive tomorrow morning. 

I can't wait to experience this, this is the first time I'm buying headphones from this pricerange. I'm both excited and scared about this buy, because of the amount I spent on it. But I have my trust in Sennheiser as I had it before with their cheaper headphones too (for my youth the HD300 blew away the competition in that range when I had little to no money).
Finally one that can connect to PC with wireless dongle and still support the microphone. Might genuinely be a great choice for calls at work and the bus home afterwards as well
Anyone have experience with how comfortable these are with glasses?

Talking about large ears in the first three minutes, you are my hero. You saved me so much curiosity and frustration after these other "rave reviews," thank you so much.
Someone's gotta topple the shp9500 for size comfort some day, no?.....

You know a headphone is great when the reviewer has to discount the app functionality to reasonably compare it to the MGs. Senny really said "heres a headphone we made to sound objectively great, but if you want some funk weve given you the capability"

one thing I've noticed as a 50yo man is that about 75% of my pals demonstrably prefer indulging in menus surrounding a potential experience rather than commencing with said experience, now they will deny this and it is merely my experience but at I've got older I've just had to quietly acquiesce to indulging friends as they just make that seventh final adjustment to the skin of their game character before commencing...or suggest looking at the Sci fi category when choosing a film then maybe fantasy or how about a comedy before returning to the original choice, if there is still time to view it, that is....creating an intuitive headphones app so the user can infinitely adjust their own sound profile is a genius move when u consider 75% of all techy men definitely prefer menus to the actual experience and I commend sennheiser for leading the way 😂
Is there also a review of the QC Ultra Gen 2 and/or the Px8S2 coming, or can i stop hoping? :D Nice review! I wish the earcups were bigger. My ear is touching everything. Sad!

The problem with having big pads is having bad anc. The reason xm6's anc is so good is because of its shallow pads, while i love and want good sound from my headphone but anc takes priority for me. I have my iems for sound quality

Agajn this is just my choice, i wish the xm series sounded a bit better and had proper eq functions like senny

Sorry but what about call quality? Did not see that in the video or lost it somehow. As I think for many many users of TWS call quality is a great factor that many manufactures do not give the appropriate care.
As someone who recently lost hearing in one ear, crossfeed that's built into the headphones and not reliant on the source device having a "mono" mode sounds great to me. Pun not intended

Great review, I’m loving mine. Best BT headphones I’ve heard. I recently had some PX8S2’s but returned them as I found the mids a little too scooped and the balance seemed off, favouring the left-May have been a bad unit. The HDB630s have an amazing level of detail, comparable to my HD650s, especially over USB when listening to lossless codecs. Just a pleasure to listen to, haven’t even tried the EQ yet. Listening via the BT700 AptX Adaptive isn’t bad either but not as smooth as USB DAC mode. My only feedback would be to add a gain setting to the app (volume can be low on USB mode for some tracks). Also would have preferred some more physical buttons and a bit more ear cup space but I’m nitpicking. Overall I’m very happy, well done Sennheiser 👌🏻

@theheadphoneshow, great review but can you also guess us your feedback on the battery life of these wireless headphones? My experience are the batteries generally have a limited shelf life (constant charge and discharge) but i have yet to see a wired pair that dies beyond everyday wear and tear. Would you still choose a wireless over a wired pair of headphones?

Worth hype imo replaced my XM5s and is head and shoulders better. Especially when using the dongle in conjunction with the iPhone 15plus. I put them on over my beanie they’re so giving and my head is huge. When worn properly my ears aren’t particularly small and have detached lobes they’re very comfortable. Guess I’m on the upper range size wise? For me when eq’d is beyond ‘exceptional’ even so out the box. Bordering on a perfect listening  headphone for me. Monitoring no but I clean the treble sufficiently with the eq for me as I despise sibilance. The option to tweak 8k is great especially for me.
I've been using that dongle with my Sony XM4's, made a big difference on my laptop
Resolve is indeed one of the reviewers of all time.
These headphones sound incredible and I've heard and owned quite a few different brands of headphones. Big Sennheiser fan however so if you like the way they eq you'll love these.

The one thing this is missing to be the ultimate “do everything” headphones is mic quality. Bluetooth is just too limited in bandwidth to do good audio both ways, and if they had the dongle doing 2.4ghz with something like a detachable boom arm, that would be perfect.

I also know this sounds a bit niche on the surface, but in a world where people are sat at their desks 8 hours a day on video calls, it’s genuinely a massive selling point.
Imagine these cups mounted to the fantastic HD 490 headband 😱

Had this for a week, and agree it has a pretty good stock tuning, and the EQ lets you fine tune it a bit more for your liking (for me, bit less bass, bit more treble). 
It was pretty nice to see how flexible the EQ is with this given I recall owning a momentum TWS before, and they gave you three bands to work with. Three. Not three points on the parametric EQ, just three bands.

Tried out Px7 S3 and the Bathys (not MG) at the store along with it and for me Px7 had way too much bass and Bathys was a bit sibilant, but both felt like they had better ANC than the HDB 630 imo so yeah the ANC is only just "ok".

Also, the dongle was pretty useful to include - helps out anyone with Apple phones, and also Samsung phones like mine because Samsung in their infinite wisdom pulled an Apple and decided not to support anything Aptx HD and newer
I was fast on the ordering and there were only a few left in Sweden, i ordered a week ago
AKG N9:  balanced signature, wifi dongle, good EQ app. Went totally unnoticed…
How do they compare to Audeze Maxwell in terms of sound quality...?

ranked.title: Sennheiser HDB 630 Headphone Review - Almost Perfect?
ranked.description:
The highly anticipated Sennheiser HDB 630 is here!  With improvements to ANC, parametric EQ, and a nifty dongle inclusion, the HDB 630 is a compelling offering.  In this review, I cover sound quality, comfort, features, connection support, and more!

And you also get an airplane adapter included — to my ears they deliver cleaner detail, better dynamics, and a more natural overall presentation. The kicker is value: my N9s are around €270, roughly half the cost, yet they still sound better in my setup. Not trying to take anything away from the 630s (build and comfort are nice); just sharing another data point from someone who compares gear a lot.

ranked.transcript: Today's an exciting day because the embargo has officially lifted on the new Sennheiser HDB 630. It's kind of a mouthful to say, but it basically is a higherend, newer version of the Momentum 4, which is a Bluetooth wireless headphone from Sennheiser with active noise cancellation and quite a few other features. In addition to the improved sound quality, there are a couple new feature enhancements that are worth talking about as well. One of them is the inclusion of the BTD700, which is a USBC transmitter, and that allows you to take advantage of AEX lossless and adaptive on a USB port. So, if your computer doesn't have the best Bluetooth adapter built in, or you just use Windows and you can't stand the Bluetooth implementation of it, this dongle makes it very simple and easy to use. Because it's a wireless transmitter, I had to do my infamous toilet test. And no, I didn't drop the transmitter in the toilet. I actually plugged it in and walked around my house to see how far it can go. And I'm pleased to say I got over 50 ft consistently with this adapter. And that's going through a couple walls and there was a wireless access point between me and the transmitter. So pretty good performance there. The other huge feature enhancement with this, this is like the game changer type thing and what a lot of people are excited about is the inclusion of parametric EQ. This is huge because Sennheiser does make good sounding products, but giving you the keys to the kingdom and allowing you to custom tune it to your personal preference is absolutely massive. Typically with a lot of headphones, you get either no EQ adjustment or basic adjustments like bass, mids, and treble or just bass and treble or sometimes these odd settings where it's like different presets like classic and hi-fi, etc. So, it varies a lot by product. The parametric EQ is incredibly powerful. All you have to do is open the app and once you're connected to the headphone, you have the ability to choose through several presets and even some of them enable bass boost if you somehow want more bass because this has gobs of it. Or when you go into custom, you can really dial it in. Now, you can go through a sound test to basically fine-tune it based off some automated stuff. Or you can do the 10B equalizer or really go for it and go for the parametric EQ. Parametric EQ isn't for the faint of heart. If you don't know what the numbers mean, I have a video that kind of explains how parametric EQ works and it loosely translates to this as well. Basically, you have the ability to adjust plus or minus 6 dB. You can do up to five points of parametric EQ. So, not a full 10 band parametric EQ, but five bands is quite good here because it sounds so good out of the box, you don't necessarily have to adjust as much. You can always stack these points. So, if you want band one and band two to focus on bass and you set it for, let's just say 60 and 65 Hz with a wide Q, well, yeah, you can lift the bass quite a bit. Naturally, you can change the Q factor, which is the biggest advantage of parametric EQ in addition to choosing your frequency because now you can choose how widely or narrowly you adjust that frequency you're targeting. They also make it very easy to confirm what your changes are. Basically, you can press and hold to bypass your parametric EQ. This is an important thing to call out though. When you do a lot of parametric EQ and you're boosting a lot of frequencies, it's going to automatically reduce your preamp. So, uh they they do this to reduce distortion because if you overboost everything on a digital signal, you're going to start having signal clipping and you introduce a lot of fake distortion. Distortion on the signal side, not the drivers themselves. So, if you play at a low volume, it can still sound distorted. Thankfully, Sennheiser takes that guesswork out by automatically reducing your preamp gain. But, as a result, if you go too aggressive on these peaks, keep in mind you are going to lose your overall peak volume. And if this reduces to, let's say, minus 8 dB on the preamp, it may not get as loud as you'd expect. So, just keep that in mind. Thankfully, Sennheiser also allows you to manually adjust the preamp to a safe level. If you're boosting a lot of frequencies, you can't bring the preamp all the way to zero, but at least you can bring some of that back and kind of work in that little gray area of what's acceptable for distortion. Another super cool trick with this is that whole tap to bypass the EQ, so you can compare your EQ profile to the stock sound profile. It matches the preamp gain. There are a lot of products out there that allow you to enable or disable parametric EQ. The problem is when you enable parametric EQ, usually you also enable that negative preamp so you don't have distortion issues. By changing the volume from toggling parametric EQ on and off, it makes it really hard to hear which sound profile you like more because amplitude can change your perception of sound and frequency response. So, this is a really nifty feature because when you toggle it on and off by doing that press and hold, you're still keeping the preamp volume at the same setting, making it really easy to compare. The tricks don't stop there because they also added crossfeed, which is what's used on the legendary H1. And there's a lot of other products implementing crossfeed now as well. But this is nice to see on a Bluetooth headphone. It kind of enhances the stereo effect to make it sound more like speakers, but it your mileage is going to vary and so will your preferences. I typically leave it off or sometimes I'll play with low, but with high I lose some of the preciseness that I got on the imaging that I was looking for and it affected bass response a little bit as well. There's a lot to like from the ergonomics in design, and I wanted to bring out the original Momentum 4 that I purchased. This was sent to me for review, by the way, and it won't affect what I say about it, but um I always want to disclose that. So, if you have a Momentum 4 or you just want to see the difference, the biggest change with the HDB 630 is the finish. You obviously get a silver yolk versus a black one. The cups are also a little bit thicker on the HDB uh 630. You can notice that the headband design is obviously different. On the Momentum 4, you get this textile fabric. I liked the headband on this. I never really had a problem with it. Uh this is an artificial leather, so it can look more premium. Uh but it's more of a personal preference. One thing I do like that they've done, you can see that this is a little bit softer here, but ultimately the headband uh is pretty consistent in terms of padding, and it kind of bottoms out right about there where I get no padding on the Moment uh HTB 630. the padding is a little bit thicker and there's also just a little bit of a detent right there in the center and that helps with relieving some pressure making it more comfortable to wear on the headband side. Now, I did measure slightly less clamp force with the HDB 630 compared to the Momentum 4. And just to show you how this compares, I also wanted to show you the clamp force of the new Bose QC Ultra uh the Quiet Comfort Ultra Gen 2 and the Foccal Batiste because I'll be making some uh comparisons to those headphones in a little bit. Clamp force is great. The weight isn't bad. It's just over 300 g, so it's kind of in the middle of weight. The Foccal Batiste is noticeably heavier, but it's not horrible. It's still under 400 g. And I use that one a lot for travel. Bose still has the uh reigning champ specs here in terms of the overall chassis design between a light clamp force and low chassis weight. And that leads me into the ear pads. The pad dimensions are basically the exact same as the Sennheiser Momentum 4, but it is quite different than both the Foccal Batiste and the Bose QC Ultras. The QC Ultras have almost the same height and width in terms of pad clearance around your ear, but they're about five millimeters deeper towards the back. And this is the same issue I had with the Momentum 4, where just the shape of this pad being the size that they are and the depth that they have, it basically puts just a little bit of pressure behind my ear. If you shrink the headband down a little bit so it's not like resting down on the top of your ear, I feel comfort improves. Now, this is kind of like what Sony did by making the distance to your ear as short as possible. You typically improve A&C performance, which I'll touch on here in just a moment, but from a comfort standpoint, there's no getting around that. My ears are making contact in several places that normally I don't prefer just because I don't like pressure behind my ear. Thankfully, everything's padded well. The baffle is padded, so that helps a lot too because this isn't like a hard plastic right here. This makes a bit of a difference on comfort. And uh this is obviously soft. It is an ear pad. So I think comfort is going to be the biggest question mark depending on your ear shape. But a lot of ANC headphones typically have a more shallow pad depth because they're trying to maximize that performance. You can clearly see the Foccal Batiste has monstrous ear pads which is a part of why they don't have the best active noise cancellation performance. But the ear clearance here is absolutely monstrous. And as a result, if you have larger ears, something like the Batist is going to be a little bit better for you. As far as some other specs go, battery life is something where Sennheiser has traditionally killed it and there's no difference here. You get 60 hours of battery life with the A&C enabled, which is absolutely massive because the other headphones typically get anywhere from 24 to 30 hours depending on the setting. Not bad battery life by any means, but having double the battery life is quite big. The other thing worth noting is this charges really fast because you can fully charge it in 2 hours, whereas the other headphones will take one and a half to two hours to get effectively half the battery life. So, the charge rate is really good on this as well. Now, because you have so many different connection options with the HDB 630, I wanted to cover a couple very unique behaviors that's worth noting in case you're shopping for this for gaming or other specific applications, mainly with use of the microphone. For one, if you use this on a PlayStation 5, you have three different ways you can try using it. You can use the included dongle, which is going to have Bluetooth and wireless connection, which is all nice. However, the default mode when you plug it in goes to basically the hands-free profile mode because it's using the protocol that supports the microphone because it's a gaming headset. This makes the audio sound absolutely terrible. And frankly, it's pretty unusable. There's kind of a trick to address this. Now, if you press the front of the transmitter four times in quick secession, it changes the mode of the transmitter. And it did restore the bandwidth I needed to get much better sound quality on my PS5, which was great. However, the microphone won't work in that case. not the end of the world because you can use the controller, but my latency wasn't the greatest either. So, even when I was playing Gran Turismo, my shifts were off sometimes just because that half second delay was noticeable. It definitely didn't hit the 30 milliseconds I was expecting. I don't know if it's just the way it negotiated with my console, but uh to me it wasn't the most usable on the wireless side. Unfortunately, even when I switched to a USBC cable and plugged it directly into the console, I had the exact same problems. it was stuck in that hands-free profile mode. And even if I disabled the microphone on the headset, so on PlayStation's audio settings, I set it to use the controller's microphone and not th

Hello guys, 
HERE TO COMPARE THE SOUND OF HDB 630, PX8 S2 and H100 (added later). 

I know there are many people like me out there wondering and trying to understand how Sennheiser HDB 630 and Bowers & Wilkins PX8 S2 sound, going through all the comments present.

Who am I? I am a kind of guy who loves listening to good, quality sounding music, definitely not a sound nerd though. I listen to mostly pop and hip-hop, and I love loud bassy musics a lot especially working out.

Sennheiser has so far never disappointed me with their products soundwise. I love this brand, they're like Mazda cars to me. However, I am foreign to B&Ws.

So, I bought them both. And here are my impressions:

HOW THEY SOUND (on Samsung S24 Ultra) 

I've switched to HDB 630 after Momentum 4 which I've really loved. HDB 630 sounds really clear, proportionate, and no distortions at high volumes contrary to Momentum 4. I just needed to turn on 'Bass Boost' which I've always done. HDB 630 sounds a little louder with the BTD 700 bluetooth dongle than without them, BUT it's still not really loud, I just feel I need more volume, especially doing sports I need that extra 'pump'. This is my ONLY BIG GRIPE.
The cups barely vibrate on the HDB 630 whereas on Momentum 4s cups really physically vibrated. Idk if you guys like this vibration feeling or not. 

 
On the other hand, B&W PX8 S2 also sounds good but not as good as HDB 630. They sound more artificial compared with HDB 630. HDB 630 feels like you're in the middle of real a stage, feels spacious, sounds are more separate, and this was also true with B&O H95. However, PX8 S2 feels rather like 'really big earbuds'. You feel the sound compacted to your ears. I can say they sound similar to Sennheiser Momentum TW 4.

PX8 S2 nails your head and tires your brain while HDB 630 soft-brushes your ear. 

With PX8 S2 I hear rather 'sh', 'ch' kind of sounds as in Russian or German languages while HDB 630 sounds really neutral, and it'd be Standart American English :D. I even sometimes find myself squinting my eyes unintentionally because those sh-ch tones in the vocals really scratch my ear like needles. 

Bass is strong but spiky on PX8 S2, has a sharp curve and you feel the cups physically vibrating similar to but less than with Momentum 4. HDB 630 has deep and sweet bass but just not loud enough which is my ONLY BIG ISSUE with it. And yes, PX8 S2 has louder maximum volume, like Momentum 4 did, with or without BTD 700 dongle than HDB 630. 

SO...
So, soundwise talking, if HDB 630 had louder maximum volume and a little stronger bass, my pick NO DOUBT would be HDB 630, ONLY IF.
I am not a fan of EQing, never needed EQing on any Sennheisers. Besides, EQing HDB 630 even causes lower volume. With PX8 S2 I needed slight EQing for my taste, but still sounding artificial compared to HDB 630. 

Plus, I had multiple chances to try B&O H95, they look classy, they sounded really clear but lacked bass and sounded really low on Samsung S10 Plus phone at the time which is a dealbreaker for me for wireless.

These have been my impressions. Hope this helps. Still hard to pick one AFTER ALL presently.

EDIT: I'm adding my B&O H100 impressions now because I had chances to try them. And yes, they fixed the low volume issue on H95. They are definitely better sounding than HDB 630 and PX8 S2, maybe slightly, maybe more. I love how separate and clear vocals are from the musical instruments unlike PX8 S2 which blends the vocals a bit, and HDB 630 are in between. Bass is really enough for me and for almost anyone, they're thumping and it feels bass is coming from far, not near your ears, like subwoofers located in a proper position. And less bass distortion on high volumes than PX8 S2. Those headphones took like twenty seconds to get to the conclusion that they are the one of the best on almost all aspects, at least for me better than HDB 630 and PX8 S2. I didn't feel I have to play with EQ settings depending on the songs as with HDB 630, whereas with PX8 S2 I looked for slight changes on the EQ from song to song. 

SOOO.... Though H100s are very much to my liking and loud, if you consider yourself "a calm, not-loud listener" unlike me, you can't go wrong with HDB 630, you won't need/look for H100. 
Or if you already have, say, Momentum 4, HDB 630 is not a must, believe me, just keep them until you see the next release. 
And if you have PX8 already, I don't think PX8 S2 is really a worth upgrade at all. 
(ONLY talking soundwise)

Thank you for the perspective. I have both the HDB 630s and PX8 S2s. There is more that needs to be discussed on these than FR charts. They provide guidance but don't convey the human side of listening. Yes the sound on the PX8 S2 is more "detailed" and has a larger soundstage but the sound profile changes significantly between ANC OFF & ON. Where your impressions with ANC ON for the PX8s? Also detail isnt everything and sound is very personal. The timbre on instruments is very often more accurate on the 630s and this adds up to an experience where the music just sounds right (the neutral tuning of the 630s). Yes you can hear more details but when a guitar string sounds metallic (PX8) vs a warmer sound there is a difference. 

Last, the 630s sound barely or at all changes when you switch from ANC OFF to Adaptive / ON to Transparency. As mentioned above, there is significant changes with the PX8 S2s. The PX8 S2s sound best with ANC Off. 

Its important people know this in a review specially for ANC headphones.

ranked.transcript: HDB 630은 기본적으로 Momentum 4의 업그레이드 버전입니다. 새로운 파라메트릭 EQ가 탑재되어 원하는 주파수 대역과 Q-팩터 조정을 정밀하게 조정할 수 있습니다 . 완벽하지는 않지만, 그래프에 최대 ±12dB까지 조정할 수 있다고 나와 있습니다. 실제로는 DSP가 개입하여 과도한 조정을 방지하기 때문에 ±3dB 까지만 조정할 수 있는 것처럼 보입니다. EQ에 대해서는 나중에 더 자세히 설명하겠습니다. 크로스피드 모드도 있습니다. 이게 뭐냐고요 ? 좌우 스테레오 채널을 중앙에 배치하는 기능입니다. 크로스피드 모드는 고가의 젠하이저 HE-1 에서 차용한 기능으로 , 스테레오 초창기, 즉 사람들이 좌우 분리형 채널을 실험 하던 시절 에 마스터링된 아주 오래된 음악을 위해 만들어졌습니다 . 데이비드 보위의 "Space Oddity" 같은 곡이 떠오르네요. 크로스피드 모드는 두 채널을 혼합하여 현대인의 취향에 더 자연스럽게 들리도록 도와줍니다. HDB 630은 USB Type-C 및 3.5mm 유선, Bluetooth, BTD 700 동글에서 연결하는 등 4가지 연결 방법이 있습니다 . 이 동글을 사용하는 주된 이유는 어떤 장치에 연결하든 APTX Adaptive Stream을 얻을 수 있다는 것입니다. iPhone은 APTX를 전혀 지원하지 않기 때문에 iPhone 사용자 에게 매우 좋습니다 . Auracast 스트리밍 도 가능하며 저지연 게임 모드 도 있습니다. 이제 다양한 연결 간에 음질 차이가 있는데 , HDB 630의 음질을 더 자세히 살펴보면서 논의 하겠습니다 . 또한 B&W PX7 S3, PX8 S2, Melomania P100 SE 및 물론 Sennheiser Momentum 4 와도 성능을 비교할 것입니다. Amazon 에서 최신 가격을 확인 하려면 고정된 댓글의 링크를 클릭하고 Shopee로 이동하려면 여기를 클릭 하고 이와 같은 리뷰를 더 보려면 구독하고 알림 종 모양 버튼을 탭하세요 . 마이크 품질 측면에서 HDB 630은 이 비교에서 가장 좋은 마이크 픽업을 가지고 있습니다 . 소음이 많은 환경에서 Momentum 4와 비교했을 때, 노이즈 게이팅이 더 깊어서 배경이 더 조용하고, 음성 필터링은 이 영상 의 다른 모델보다 더 잘 작동하는 것 같습니다 . 하지만 HDB 630 의 노이즈 게이팅이 매우 소음이 많은 환경에서 매우 매우 공격적이라는 것도 알아차렸습니다. 곧 보실 것과 마찬가지로, 너무 공격적 이어서 제 목소리의 일부가 잘려나갑니다. 다른 것들과 마찬가지로, 노이즈 게이팅은 그렇게 공격적이지 않습니다 . 하지만 동시에 노이즈 게이팅이 그렇게 공격적이지 않기 때문에 마이크를 통해 들어오는 많은 배경 소음이 들어오고 제 목소리도 묻힙니다. 어떻게든 제 목소리가 HDB 630을 통해 들리는 방식이 더 좋습니다 . 하지만 여러분은 어떻게 생각하시나요? 댓글로 알려주세요 . 이것은 정말 큰 카페 소음 과 이 선풍기의 바람 소음을 사용하여 조용하고 소음이 많은 조건 에서 테스트되었습니다 . 액티브 노이즈 캔슬링 측면에서 HDB630은 쇼핑몰 의 배경 소음 부터 아내와 아이들 의 목소리, 대중교통의 저주파 소음까지 일상적인 소음을 효과적으로 차단합니다 . 보스 QC Ultra Gen 2나 소니 WH-1000XM6 같은 최고의 노이즈 캔슬링 제품들만큼 소음을 차단하지는 못할 수도 있습니다 . 특히 고주파 영역에서는 더욱 그렇습니다. 하지만 이 비교 대상 제품들과 비교하면 HDB630 은 최고의 노이즈 캔슬링 제품 중 하나입니다. B&W PX7S3만이 HDB630보다 약간 더 조용합니다. 샘플을 들어보세요. 이제 액티브 노이즈 캔슬링과 통화 품질 을 다른 제품과 비교하고 싶다면 , 영어: 한 달에 1달러 이상만 내면 내 개인 데이터베이스 인 MY SECRET WEAPON에 접속할 수 있는데, 여기서 샘플 A와 B를 직접 비교할 수 있습니다. 수백 명의 사람들이 가입했는데, 이는 모든 마케팅을 건너뛰고 매장을 방문하지 않고도 직접 제품을 비교할 수 있어 많은 시간을 절약할 수 있기 때문입니다. 대부분의 매장에서는 위생상의 이유로 제품을 시도해 볼 수조차 없습니다 . 아래 링크를 통해 지금 YouTube나 Patreon에 가입하세요 . 투명 모드의 경우, 일반적인 인식 에 충분히 투명 해서 꽤 사용하기 편리하고 , 사람들과 이야기할 때 내 목소리 가 가려지는 느낌이 들지 않았습니다. 그래서 언어 적 교환이 매우 자연스럽게 느껴졌습니다. 이 비교에서 P100 SE보다 투명 하고 저주파에서 이전 Momentum 4보다 약간 더 깨끗합니다. 하지만 투명 모드에서 B&W 헤드폰만큼 명확하지 않거나 통풍이 잘 되지 않습니다 . Bluetooth 및 기본 설정에서 의 사운드 품질에 대해 Momentum 4와 비교한다면 업그레이드입니다. 다이내믹 레인지, 튜닝 및 사운드 스테이징 측면에서 HDB630은 Momentum 4 와 비슷한 사운드를 내지 만 Momentum 4보다 분해능이 더 뛰어나고 개별 트랙의 선명도와 초점이 더 뛰어납니다.하지만 더 비판적인 청취자에게는 HDB630이 최고의 사운드 품질을 가지고 있다고 말하지 않을 것입니다 . 특히 이 비교에서 B&W 및 Cambridge 오디오 헤드폰과 비교한다면 더욱 그렇습니다.B &W PX7 S3 및 PX8 S2와 비교했을 때 HDB 630의 사운드는 매우 다릅니다.더 편안하고 느긋하며 중음역에서 더 강조 되고 저음역 에서 덜 붐비는 소리가 납니다 .사운드 스테이징도 더 중앙에 있는 느낌이지만 B&W는 좌우 요소가 더 측면에 있습니다. 따라서 매우 다른 경험입니다. 나는 그것을 느긋하고 부드러운 사운드 의 HDB 630과 비교했을 때 더 매력적이고 포용적이라고 설명할 것입니다. HDB 630과 비교했을 때 B&W가 더 나은 사운드를 내는 이유는 중역대 에 더 집중력이 있고 상위 및 하위 하모닉스에서 더 잘 분해되기 때문 입니다. 예를 들어 HDB 630에서 기타 줄을 뜯는 소리를 매우 선명하게 들을 수 있지만 PX7 S3와 PX8 S2에서 같은 기타 줄을 뜯는 소리 만큼 바디감이나 공기감이 많지 않습니다. 이제 두 제품 중에서 PX8 S2가 가장 좋은 사운드를 냅니다. 가장 잘 분해되기 때문에 다양한 트랙과 악기에 집중력과 사실감이 더해집니다 . 또한 HDB 630에 비해 저음 에 더 많은 질감이 있고 더 깊은 펀치감을 주며 PX7 S3보다 덜 붐비기 때문에 PX8 S2가 이 그룹에서 가장 좋은 사운드를 낸다고 말합니다. Sennheiser가 두 B&W보다 더 나은 점 은 레이어링 측면에서입니다. 사운드 스테이지에 그라데이션이 더 많아 음악의 배경과 전경을 구분하기가 더 쉽고 , 제가 꽤 좋아하는 더 부드럽고 편안한 사운드 시그니처라고 생각합니다. 중음역대 에 대한 강조는 보컬의 음색을 더 강조합니다. Melomania P100 SE와 Sennheiser HD 630은 모두 느긋하고 차분한 사운드 헤드폰이지만, HDB 630은 Melomania보다 사운드 스테이지가 약간 더 편안 하여 중음역대에 더 집중되어 사운드 스테이지의 주변부가 약간 더 차분하게 느껴집니다. 그러나 P100 SE는 더 해상도가 높습니다. 트랙은 더 선명하게 초점이 맞춰지고 악기는 약간 더 공기감이 있으며 HDB 630보다 레이어링이 약간 더 많아 사운드 스테이지에 3D 품질이 약간 더 많고 홀로그래피가 약간 더 있습니다. P100 SE를 듣고 있습니다. 어쨌든, 이 헤드폰을 비교하는 사운드 샘플을 몇 개 녹음해 봤습니다 . 한번 들어보세요. 다른 헤드폰이나 이어버드와 음질을 비교하려면 loudnwireless.com/soundsamples 를 방문하세요 . 기기를 HDB 630에 연결하는 방식에 따라 소리가 약간 다를 수 있습니다. 제가 자세히 설명해 드리겠습니다. 블루투스로 연결했을 때는 가장 차분하고 저음이 가장 많이 감쇄되는 소리가 납니다. BTD 700 동글을 사용하면 중고음과 고음이 약간 더 선명해지고 블루투스보다 중음이 더 강조되는 것을 느낄 수 있습니다. USB Type-C 케이블로 연결했을 때는 중고음과 고음이 블루투스보다 감쇄되지만 다이내믹 레인지가 조금 더 넓습니다. 그리고 3.5mm 케이블을 통해 USB Type C와 비슷한 소리가 나지만 저음은 USB C에 비해 약간 더 크게 들립니다. 모든 연결 방법은 최대 볼륨 레벨에서 거의 비슷 하지만 Bluetooth에서 50% 볼륨 에서 HDB630은 다른 연결 방법보다 약 10dB 더 부드럽습니다 . 이것은 기본 EQ에 있습니다. 이제 파라메트릭 이퀄라이저 로 조정하면 마스터 게인을 최대로 올려도 볼륨이 약 10% 더 떨어질 것으로 예상할 수 있습니다. 제 생각에는 그러한 균형이 매우 가치 있는 이유는 사운드에서 수정하고 싶은 것이 하나 있다면 저음을 더 추가하고 중음의 밝기를 줄이고 고음 의 밝기를 더하여 사운드 스테이지를 공기감 있게 찾는 것이기 때문입니다. 이것이 제가 도달한 EQ 설정입니다. 자유롭게 복사하세요. 이제 B&W에 더 가깝게 들리지만 여전히 느긋하고 겹겹이 쌓인 시그니처를 유지합니다. 편안함을 위해 쿠션은 Momentum 4에 비해 머리에 조금 더 푹신하게 느껴지 지만 그 외에는 매우 비슷합니다. 클램핑 압력도 P100 SE와 B&W 헤드폰보다 더 부드럽습니다. 그래서 자주 휴식을 취할 필요는 없지만 이어컵이 너무 얕다 고 생각합니다 . 그래서 저처럼 귀가 크다면 스크림에 눌리는 느낌이 들 것 입니다. 첫째, 둘째 , 열이 더 빨리 쌓여서 꽤 빨리 땀이 납니다. 또 다른 개선된 영역은 630을 두 대의 장치에 동시에 연결하면 동글을 꽂았을 때 자동으로 동글로 전환 되지 않는다는 것입니다. 먼저 한 장치에서 연결을 끊은 다음 동글에서 페어링을 시작해야 하므로 프로세스가 느려집니다. 그 외에도 HDB 630은 매우 인상적입니다. 네, 꽤 비싸지 만 훌륭한 전반적인 성능, 훌륭한 앱, 사용자 정의 기능 및 별도로 구매할 필요가 없는 BTD 700 동글을 고려할 때 확실히 옵션입니다 .Amazon 에서 최신 가격을 확인하려면 고정된 댓글의 링크를 클릭하고 Shopee로 이동하려면 여기를 클릭하세요. 이 채널의 향후 리뷰를 놓치고 싶지 않으시다면 구독 하고 종 모양 버튼을 눌러 알림을 받으세요. 또한, 이 영상을 시청하고 어떤 헤드폰과 이어버드가 최고의 선택인지 알아보세요 .

judgement.community_consensus: Sennheiser HDB 630은 Momentum 4의 업그레이드로, 사운드 품질과 ANC 성능이 개선되었으나 볼륨 부족과 베이스 강도가 주요 단점으로 지적된다. 사용자들은 파라메트릭 EQ를 통해 사운드를 조정하는 것을 추천하며, B&W PX8 S2나 Melomania P100 SE와 비교해 더 부드럽고 편안한 사운드를 선호한다.

reviewer_claims: HDB 630은 Momentum 4의 업그레이드 버전이며 사운드 품질이 개선되었다.
파라메트릭 EQ를 통해 사운드를 정밀하게 조정할 수 있다.
ANC 성능은 비교 대상 제품 중 상위권이다.
B&W PX8 S2보다 부드럽고 편안한 사운드를 제공한다.
USB Type-C 및 3.5mm 유선, Bluetooth, BTD 700 동글을 통해 다양한 연결 옵션이 제공된다.

5:29 ‘Gaming mode’ is a feature of the dongle that’s not supported by this headphone, so unfortunately you won’t get 30ms of latency. According to Sennheiser’s FAQ the best case scenario would be AptX Adaptive with 80ms latency - which quite noticable for gaming. The same is true for AptX lossless - it’s supported by the dongle, but not the headphone, so no lossless wireless audio. It’s unfortunate that @Sennheiser watched and commented on this video, without correcting these small mistakes.

When that violin first come in on that high note.....the emotion that washes over you is incredible. I have no way to describe it.

I would definitely be interested in trying these out in the future once my Bose break.

Noooo, I can't believe Sennheiser jumped on the touch control train. Having physical buttons on the headphones is leagues better, without accidental touch registers or ambiguity, and there were so few companies that still kept it. Extremely disappointing.

I love the angle that Jake is going for here, but there is a big difference between frequency response and technical performance. The frequency graph may be on par or better than the HE 1, but there will always be a difference between closed-back and open-back headphones, as well as the driver type (dynamic on these vs. the electrostatic on the HE 1s). Things like soundstage, stereo imaging, input audio quality etc. will always be something to consider.

That being said, these headphones are definitely a step in the right direction for most people, and it's great that Sennheiser is opening up the field for this sort of discussion when other companies like Apple & Bose have been chasing their own vision of what "sounds the best".

- Is the battery replaceable?

- Can the ear pads be easily replaced?

Something that is incredibly important when it comes to headphones that CANNOT be represented by a frequency response graph is sound stage. That is something that you can describe with words, but can really only be understood after experiencing it. The biggest difference between something like these and the HE-1 is not necessarily how well they can recreate specific frequencies, but how they are portrayed to your ears. I personally have not experienced the HDB 630 or HE-1, but having listened to my fair share of other consumer level and audiophile level headphones, having similar frequency response graphs and a gigantic price gap doesn't make the HDB 630's an obvious buy if your intention is to sit, listen to music, and have your mind blown. That being said, I am sure the HDB 630's are great (that USB adapter really does look like a game changer for on the go listening), I just wish more of the experience of headphones were talked about in these kinds of videos.

TLDR: The HDB 630's are probably great, but cant really be compared to the HE-1's.

Bluetooth LE actually has an obscure profile called the Gaming Audio Profile (GMAP) that is able to provide fullband stereo two-way low latency audio. Which means audio doesn't get drastically downgraded anymore if you have voice calls on (no more mono audio during a voice call). Not much documentation publicly available, and the headphones that do support it aren't always clear that they do. So far, the only source devices that have this feature are the BTD700 dongle shown in this video, the Creative Sound blaster BT-W6 and the FlooGoo FMA120, but with somewhat spotty implementation (BT-W6 is known to not work with non-QCC Bluetooth chips even though GMAP is supposedly an open standard, but the FMA120 seems to work flawlessly from what I've read all over the internet).
I would like to see some testing on the availability of this feature for Bluetooth LE-capable Headphones. Testing doesn't have to be complicated, just check if the FMA120 allows you to turn on Gaming mode under LE and that the audio doesn't degrade to AM Radio quality while the microphone is on.

ranked.transcript: Sennheiser just dropped the Momentum 5 Wireless onto [music] us, and it's one impressive set of headphones. But, should owners of the older Momentum 4 Wireless or HD 630 rush out and buy it? Well, we thought it'd be useful to forego the usual head-to-head match-up and make things a little bit more interesting today by comparing Sennheiser's other older ANC headsets [music] to the new one, just for giggles. Before we get into it, we at SoundGuys want to show you the Loop Switch 2, [music] which is available on Amazon. This is a good option for anyone looking for some earplugs for concerts or just to quiet the world around you. You can find the link in the description below. Now, this is a paid link, so we do get compensation when you click on the link, but if you're enjoying all the free reviews and lab data, then this is a great way to support everything we do here at SoundGuys. Both the Sennheiser Momentum 4 Wireless and HD 630 share a lot of the same design elements, down to the mic array design language and band, the shapes used, and more. I mean, look at them. The Momentum 5 Wireless, on the other hand, is a much clearer-looking set of headphones than the previous two. [music] Though, it still does use the cloth band material that the Momentum 4 uses, and that gets grody over time. The new headphones have metallic plates over the windscreens on the microphones and a circular brand logo on the same friction band as the other two. Whoop-dee-doo. Who cares? But, what is exciting is that the left driver assembly is easy to remove, and the battery within is replaceable, meaning that [music] the Momentum 5 Wireless has a much longer potential operating life than the other two headphones, as you should be able to replace the battery for far cheaper than it would be to buy a new set of headphones, and that is [music] a big whoop. All three come with a zipper case with hard plastic inside. All three have removable padding, and all three bear no ingress protection rating whatsoever. But, from a design standpoint, the Momentum 5 Wireless takes this one, no question. Connection options. This one's very close, as always, because all three headphones are extremely similar and based off of extremely similar hardware. All three headphones offer wireless and wired listening via Bluetooth, analog wired, and digital audio over USB-C. All three headphones support multipoint, and all three headphones allow you to tinker with these connection settings within the app. However, what differentiates the models is the Bluetooth version and the HD 630's use of a dongle to make any smartphone, tablet, or computer compliant with the aptX Adaptive family of codecs. Plug it in, pair it with the app, and you're listening to aptX Adaptive on an iPhone. The Momentum 4 Wireless is so old, it shows its age here quite a bit. Using Bluetooth 5.2, it does not have LE Audio features like the HD 630 and the Momentum 5 Wireless, which means that for anyone hoping to use Auracast with a newer TV, venue, or assistive technology, you're SOL there. Additionally, gamers enjoy lower latencies with LC3, which is not present on the older headphones. For this one, it's easy to see that the HD 630 takes this one on the back of its dongle. Though, the Momentum 5 Wireless being Bluetooth 6.0 ready, whenever that firmware update happens, has a lot more than just bigger number available to it. For most people, the two newer headphones win this one. Features. This is a tough comparison because all three headphones have extremely similar features, but are differentiated mainly by the equalizer. The Momentum 4 Wireless headphones have a five-band EQ, the Momentum 5 Wireless has an eight-band EQ, while the HD 630 has the option of using a parametric EQ instead. For those of you who aren't aware, this feature allows you to make very specific kinds of adjustments without a defined start and stop point if you don't wish. Because of this, enthusiasts can make their headphones sound a lot closer to what they want instead of playing a guessing game with the band EQ. Of course, it's a bit intimidating for anyone who doesn't know what they're doing, but having that option is something we'd like to see on all headphone apps. Speaking of headphone apps, all three models compared here use the same Sennheiser Smart Control Plus app. You can find your device if you lose things constantly, adjust controls, how your headphone sound, esoteric settings, control ANC or transparency mode strength, >> [music] >> you get the idea. There are some differences depending on which headphone you use. For example, the Momentum 5 Wireless is the only one of the three that supports Dolby Atmos with head tracking as [music] of June 3rd. The HD 630 wins this one, however, but it's not an overwhelming victory or anything. Battery life. Now, when it comes to battery life, crowning a winner here is absolute folly. All three of these headphones handily eclipse 50 hours with ANC on for playback. So, it really doesn't benefit anybody to pick a winner here. However, the main thing is is that with the user replaceable battery of the Momentum 5 Wireless, the headphones will have a much longer operating life as I mentioned earlier. That alone means that this is the better battery. Microphone quality. Microphone quality is tough to show on YouTube because it takes time. And the results you hear anyone using a product out in their daily lives might not apply to you. For example, you might not be a 20-to-30-year-old dude with vocal fry living in New York City. Because it takes a lot of effort to meet more people's needs, we record samples that are quite a bit more exhaustive for our readers on the site. You think that this would give us a lot of material to put in a video, but not everyone is willing to sit through multiple 50-second long samples just to get a quick answer. The best we can do is show you where to find these samples and what to listen for. That way you can hear the most appropriate comparisons for your daily life without having to scrub through a ton of guff here. That being said, I will say that in the context of this comparison, you might find that the Sennheiser Momentum 5 Wireless does a better job of killing noise in your phone calls, and it's due in no small part to the new windscreens and newer hardware. Now, that's not to say that the older HD 630 and Momentum 4 Wireless are bad at all. They're not, but the newer headphones are slightly better at handling wind noise. That's pretty much it. Active noise cancellation. While the old axiom, newer is always better, has plenty of counter examples, headphones for one, for active noise canceling headphones, it's more or less okay in this instance. Sennheiser's ANC hasn't been as strong as the top tier competitors for quite some time, but it's crawling back. The Sennheiser Momentum 5 Wireless, the newest contestant in our brawl here, is a better set of noise cancellers than the other two. However, it only looks a little bit better on a chart, but what's going on? On Momentum 4 Wireless, we're panned for having somewhat weak ANC, and that's true, and that didn't change when the same hardware was brought to the HD [music] 630. However, it was able to achieve better overall noise attenuation because the bigger ear cups were able to make a better seal, and physically blocking more noise meant that the ANC unit didn't have to be much better to have audible improvements. But see this little region here? See how the Momentum 5 Wireless has a slight improvement in the mids? What you probably can't get from this chart is that this is an area where a lot of extra garbage in your day-to-day life lives, and being more effective here will pay dividends when you come across sounds like babies crying, church bells, the subway, you name [music] it. On paper, it's only a minor improvement, but it's the exact kind that you'll be able to hear if you get the chance to compare. Winner, Momentum 5 Wireless. Final round, sound quality. Here's where things are going to get a little contentious as all three headphones have different philosophies on what good sound is, and you heard that right. The Momentum 4 Wireless, HD 630, and Momentum 5 Wireless all try to do slightly different things. The Momentum 4 Wireless offers a sound that is slightly emphasized bass, good treble extension, and a rather inoffensive tuning for most of the range of human hearing. Of the three models listed here, it's the closest to our headphone preference curve, or what we use for context in comparing headphones. Meeting this line doesn't mean something sounds good, it's just that a lot of things that sound good often measure this way. The HD 630, on the other hand, targets a more theoretically sound response that's meant to appeal to serious listeners. It's got some bass, but not too crazy, fairly flat mids, and slightly under emphasized highs that can easily be rescued in the app. Because this tuning is close to something resembling a good baseline, it's far easier to tweak your preferences using the parametric EQ. This has led to a lot of well-earned gushing online. Finally, the Momentum 5 Wireless is uh >> [snorts] >> very much a consumer headphone. Gone is the more reasonable bass replaced with higher energy in both lows and highs. For some reason, the highest octave has a big drop in the emphasis, but older listeners may not care about this so much. There's a really weird transition on from 1 kHz on up, but overall, the headphones score fairly well. In fact, if you were to pass all three of these headphones to a group of 200 listeners, the scores would be extremely similar. I'm going to say this right off the bat that if you're extremely picky about audio quality, the feature enhancements that the Momentum 5 Wireless has are not worth it over the HD 630, and that I'd recommend the Momentum 4 Wireless and HD 630 over Sennheiser's latest headphones if we're looking only at sound quality and nothing else. While I don't begrudge a company for looking to make a new home for a product line that's more consumer-oriented by differentiating it from a recent set of audiophile ANC headphones, taking the tuning in this direction is a choice. It sounds fine, but it's definitely something I wish I had more control over to change with, say, I don't know, the parametric EQ. A drop off in the highs, wobble in the mids, and overemphasis in the lows are all features I'd like to address, but I really can't with the existing options. I'm going to say that the HD 630 wins here, but with the caveat that none of the options in this scrum sound truly bad. It's just that some sound better than others. All right, time for our match results. There are three headphones in this slap fight, each with its own advantages over the others. The Sennheiser Momentum 4 Wireless sounds better than the Momentum 5 Wireless and is much less expensive on sale. The Sennheiser HD 630 cost the most, but sounds the best, has better noise attenuation than the older Momentum 4 Wireless, and a dongle that can ensure a high-performance floor. The Sennheiser Momentum 5 Wireless sounds fine, is less expensive than the HD 630, and offers superior noise canceling. All these headphones have excellent baseline performance, and all these options have incredible battery life with ANC enabled. Really, it's hard to pick wrong here, just varying shades of what you actually need. But if you can't find the Momentum 4 Wireless on sale, and you aren't ready to sink a ton of money on the HD 630 at full price, you can check out the finer points of the Momentum 5 Wireless over here.

ranked.description:

Get the Sennheiser HDB 630: https://www.rtings.com/afflink/AxoAAcaoAAA=

With the Sennheiser HDB 630, Sennheiser’s Sonova-run consumer audio division is aiming to bring top-quality sound to the Bluetooth over-ears market. Designed to deliver balanced, accurate audio instead of a bass-heavy or V-shaped tuning, the HDB 630 make a clear play for listeners who care most about sound fidelity. They pair that focus with modern wireless features, long battery life, and active noise cancelling in a premium package. But with strong competition in this price range, the real question is whether their sound-first approach is enough to set them apart from the rest of the field. 

Model Tested: Sennheiser HDB 630

ranked.transcript: Just before the holidays, Sennheiser came out swinging with a huge release. Were this year's best sounding Bluetooth headphones released in 2025? [music] Hi, I'm Ben from ratings.com and today we're taking a look at the Sennheiser HDB 630. These wireless overears are the most premium cans we've seen from Sennheiser's Senova Run consumer audio division. But don't let the word consumer fool you. Instead of leaning into a boosted bass or V-shaped tuning, these cans promise a more balanced, accurate sound than you'd typically get from Bluetooth headphones. [music] Do these live up to the hype? Keep watching to find out. If you like what you see and want to support independent, non-sponsored, datadriven reviews, why not subscribe? It's free and it really helps us out. Since these headphones come bearing promises of superior sound quality, that's where we'll start. The HDB 630 have a balanced default sound which sets them apart from most wireless noise cancelling headphones. Instead of heavily boosting the bass, their low-end stays controlled [music] with just a slight emphasis that adds thump and punch to kick drums in tunes like Sandstorm by Darude. Baselines feel full without ever overwhelming the mix. The mid-range sits remarkably close to our target curve, so vocals and instruments sound true to life and present even in busy arrangements. Their treble is a little less controlled in the bass with a small dip in the mid treble that takes the edge off harsher sounds like symbols. Still, there's enough aireriness in the high treble to ensure these cans don't sound muffled. These headphones barely deviate from their own balanced sound profile. Outside of that, inconsistency in the treble. The left and right drivers are amazingly well matched, so objects like voices are correctly located in the stereo field. The group delay performance is also decent. There are no audible issues, so kick drums feel appropriately tight, and the transients on the snare drums are crisp. Harmonic distortion is also remarkably low, so you can blast your favorite tunes at unsafe listening levels without encountering audible distortion artifacts. As closedbacks, these headphones don't make your audio feel very open or expansive. Your favorite tunes will also sound more like they're coming from inside your head rather than two speakers in a room, but that's part for the course with this design. So, are there any real downsides to how these headphones sound? Well, yes, there is one. Like many overearc headphones, the HDB 630 can be sensitive to fit and seal. Small changes in how they sit on your head can affect the bass and lower mid-range, which means you might not always hear the exact same sound [music] every time you put them on. This is especially noticeable if you wear thicker framed glasses, which can prevent the ear cups from forming a seal. [music] If you do wear thicker frame glasses, we suggest an alternate pair of headphones for you at the end of the video. We've not yet covered one of the HDB 630's biggest strengths, the companion app. Since it offers so many sound customization options, we're lumping it into the sound section. In addition to a fiveband graphic EQ, Sennheiser includes a detailed parametric EQ that lets you adjust frequency, gain, and Q with much more precision. It's easy to tune the sound to your preferences or correct small issues without dramatically changing the overall tuning. Sennheiser even includes a personalized EQ feature called sound check, which lets you create a preset by using your own music. And [music] there's a bass boost option, too. Perhaps most interestingly for audio nerds, there's also a crossfeed option, which blends the left and right channels slightly to create a more speaker-like presentation, which can be useful if you listen to a lot of older stereo mixes with hard panning. Beyond sound adjustments, you get the standard array of ANC controls, connection management, etc. You can find a video of the app in use on our website. So, all in all, besides some lousy F FRC, these cans offer impressive audio performance across the board [music] and an app that lets you tailor their sound exactly to your liking. Oh, wait. Before we move on from sound, there's just one more thing that may be of interest to you. These headphones come bundled with a dedicated Bluetooth dongle, the BTD700. It plugs into the USBC port on your phone, computer, or PlayStation, but it won't work on Xbox, which lets you bypass your devices internal Bluetooth hardware. Audio files may appreciate this as it makes it easier to pick and choose between different codecs, bringing high-res formats to devices that don't offer native support. Sennheiser also says it allows for Bluetooth latency as low as 30 milliseconds over ax low latency, but so far the lowest we've been able to achieve is around 100 milliseconds. The HDB 630 have a clean, understated design that leads more practical than flashy. You can have them in any color so long as it's, [music] yep, you guessed it, black. Well, the metallic yolks are silver, but apart from that, they're mostly made of black matte plastic. Still, they feel well-b built and solid in hand with no obvious weak points. [music] The ear cups can swivel and lay flat, reducing their footprint when you're not using them, but they won't fold inwards. These are pretty comfortable headphones. The ear cups are well padded, so they don't feel overly tight during longer listening sessions. But if your ears are on the larger side, you may find the inside of the ear cups rub up against you. The padding at the center of the headband is also a bit thin, which might be a little uncomfortable over longer listening sessions if you have a larger head. The hinges on the ear cups rotate a full 180°, and the headband uses a smooth adjustment system, making it easy to fine-tune the fit. People with longer hair, take care when adjusting, as hair can get caught in the hinges or yolks. These headphones are fine for taking on a brisk walk, but they're not really designed to accompany you on more vigorous activities like runs or gym workouts. The HDB 630 have very good controls that are primarily touch sensitive with input gestures for playback, volume, calls, and noise control. The power button is the only physical control. All the controls are located on a single ear cup and you can't customize the input gestures in the companion app. We found the touch surface can also register accidental inputs on occasion. In terms of feedback, you get a mix of voice prompts and tones with the option to choose a language or disable voice prompts entirely. [music] Battery level, power state, and connection status are clearly communicated, and volume limits are easy to identify thanks to distinct warning tones. But the tones played when switching between A and C and transparency modes can feel a bit ambiguous. The included case is great. [music] It's large, making it better suited for packing into your carry-on than taking on your daily commute, but its hard shell structure offers good protection. Inside, you'll find custom molded cutouts to hold the headphones securely, along with dedicated spaces for cables, the USBC dongle, and other accessories. [music] The interior is lined with a soft velvet-like material, and the pouches use tight drawstrings to keep everything from shifting around. The case fully zips closed for a secure seal and includes a carrying strap. The HDB 630 have amazing noise isolation, but it's not in the same category as class leaders like the Sony WH000XM6 or Bose Quiet Comfort Ultra Headphones, second generation. That might be a little disappointing for some listeners since the HDB 630 are priced about the same as the Sony and Bose flagships, but Sennheiser have historically been a little behind in the ANC wars, and that trend continues here. Still, these headphones do an excellent job blocking out sound across most of the frequency range. While it's true the very lowest rumbles of a bus or plane engine can still creep in, and attenuation in the mid mids is weaker than competitors, most everyday noise is heavily reduced. In our tests, the ANC performed similarly whether it was set to adaptive or max. So, you don't need to worry about losing performance by choosing one mode over the other. These cans also have a feature that helps limit artifacts caused by wind noise interacting with the ANC microphones. It works better than many similar implementations, noticeably reducing wind noise when enabled. Although activating the feature slightly reduces overall ANC strength, the Sennheiser HDB 630 have a decent microphone. Your voice comes through clearly and remains easy to understand, though it sounds a bit thin and lacking in sparkle. You sound clear, if not especially lifelike. More impressive is the noise handling. In moderately noisy environments, background sounds are kept under control without overpowering your voice. [music] And even in louder settings like a subway, the mic does a solid job of suppressing surrounding noise and preventing clipping. The HDB 630 have superb battery performance. Sennheiser advertises up to 60 hours of playback, and we measured just over 54 with&c on. They fully charge in about 1.5 hours, and Sennheiser claims you can get up to 7 hours of use from a 10-minute charge. Charging is done over USBC, and you can continue listening while they're plugged in. The HDB 630 offer very flexible connectivity options. You can listen wired over USBC or via the included 3.5 mm to 2.5 mm analog cable. Though the headphones still need to be powered on when used this way since their design relies on DSP. Because of that, there's still some latency even in analog. It's low enough for watching videos, but it's not ideal for competitive gaming, and the built-in mic doesn't work over analog. The headphones support Bluetooth multipoint, so you can stay connected to two devices at once, though there's no fast pair support for Android or iOS. They also support a wide range of Bluetooth codecs, including higher bit rate options like APX adaptive high quality. So, how do these stack up against competitors on the market? Earlier in the video, we mentioned that the HDB 630 might not be the best choice for people who wear thicker frame glasses. If that's you, the Foccal Batist Wireless are the obvious alternative. They can't match the Sennheiser for battery life or noise isolation, but they have a balanced sound that's almost as refined as the HDB 630. audio files may find the Focal's 10B graphic EQ isn't as flexible as the Sennheiser's parametric EQ. If noise isolation is a priority, the Sony WH000XM6 are the cans to beat. They're the current market leaders, but their default warm bassy sound signature isn't as well balanced as the HDB 630. They also suffer from issues related to fit. Finally, if you love the idea of the HDB 630, but bulk at the price, the Sennheiser Momentum 4 wireless are the way to go. In many ways, the Sennheiser HDB 630 feel like a more premium take on the Momentum 4 with the two sharing very similar designs. The HDB 630 are pricier and deliver a more balanced sound, better noise isolation, and stronger microphone performance. But the Momentum 4 aren't that far behind in all categories, even offering a longer battery life. But you may want to tame the big boost in their bass if you prefer a more balanced sound. Out of all the wireless Bluetooth headphones we've tested, the HDB 630 come closest to our target. So, if sound quality is your top priority, these are an easy recommendation. They deliver a balanced sound, superb battery life, outstanding app support, and amazing noise isolation. Even if their ANC doesn't quite reach class leading levels. Aside from price, their main downside is that achieving a consistent fit can be [music] challenging, especially if you wear thicker frame glasses. But those aside, the HDB 630 are a very impressive pair of he
